narrative:
Short impact summary

The article titled "Early Transmission Dynamics in Wuhan, China, of Novel Coronavirus–Infected Pneumonia" has achieved significant academic impact, amassing 17,937 citations in the scholarly landscape. Backed by notable institutions like the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases and with a strong funding foundation from multiple agencies, this research has not only influenced scientific discourse but also found its way into wider public conversations.

Full narrative

Published in the New England Journal of Medicine, the work by Qun Li and colleagues has significantly shaped our understanding of the early transmission dynamics of the novel coronavirus. With 17,937 citations according to OpenAlex, it stands as a pivotal contribution to the ongoing pandemic literature. This high citation rate signals a robust academic engagement, reflecting the critical nature of the findings in shaping responses to the COVID-19 outbreak.

Moreover, the article has generated public discourse, as evidenced by its presence in various online platforms, including Wikipedia with 8 mentions and a single post on Bluesky. This dissemination beyond the academic community highlights its relevance and the public's engagement with scientific findings related to the pandemic. The crossover into public domains illustrates how academic research can resonate with broader societal concerns.

The research has strong institutional backing, being associated with key organizations such as the World Wide Web Consortium and supported by prominent funding agencies including the Ministry of Science and Technology and the National Natural Science Foundation of China. This financial and institutional validation underscores the importance of investment in research related to infectious diseases and public health.

In the context of its citation landscape, the article has significantly influenced successive scholarly work, with a notable representation of citations originating from countries such as China (708), the United States (585), and the United Kingdom (236). This geographical reach reflects a global recognition of its contributions and the urgency in addressing transmissible diseases. The work's continuous reuse and citation across various venues, particularly in journals like the Journal of Medical Virology and Clinical Infectious Diseases, further validate its relevance and impact within the scientific community.
